region_of_origin: Levant (Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, Palestine); popularised in Turkey and across the Middle East

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for arayes kofta; closest defensible match is 'stuffed flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315595). Arayes is a pita bread stuffed with spiced ground meat and grilled.

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP used as closest defensible category for stuffed/filled bread with meat. No exact arayes kofta entry exists in FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of arayes kofta is one pita bread stuffed with spiced ground lamb kofta, approximately 250 g, typically served as a main meal item.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Ground lamb, cooked (SR Legacy ID 17068) and Pita bread, white, unenriched (SR Legacy ID 18413); composite calculation for arayes kofta
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore entry exists for arayes kofta as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked ground lamb (FDC ID 174351) and white pita bread (FDC ID 175040), combined in an approximate 60:40 lamb-to-pita ratio consistent with the dish description. Input per_100g values (energy 220 kcal, protein 14 g, fat 12 g, carbohydrate 14 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained. Secondary micronutrient values (calcium 20 mg, iron 1.6 mg, cholesterol 40 mg, saturated fat 4.8 g, trans fat 0.2 g, vitamin C 2.0 mg) are plausible for this composition and have been retained from the input with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and other micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1 (data not available from composite estimation).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; white pita bread GI analogous estimate applied to arayes kofta composite
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal (peer-reviewed)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for arayes kofta. GI estimated at 57 (medium) based on white pita bread GI of approximately 57 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The substantial protein (14 g/100g) and fat (12 g/100g) from the lamb kofta filling are expected to moderate the glycaemic response relative to plain pita bread alone. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (57 × 35.0) / 100 = 19.95, where carbohydrate per 250 g serving = 14.0 × 2.5 = 35.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 280 mg/100g; estimated 700 mg per 250 g serving, approaching 30% of the 2,300 mg daily limit in a single serving
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit arayes kofta to a half-portion (approximately 125 g), request reduced salt in the spice mix, avoid high-sodium condiments such as tahini sauce, and choose whole wheat pita to increase potassium and fibre intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 14 g/100g (35 g per serving) from pita bread with medium GI 57; protein and fat from lamb kofta attenuate postprandial glycaemic rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should limit portion to one half-pita serving (approximately 125 g), request whole wheat pita to lower GI and increase fibre, monitor total carbohydrate intake, and be aware that saturated fat from lamb (4.8 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with frequent consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.8 g/100g (12 g per serving) and trans fat 0.2 g/100g from ground lamb; sodium 280 mg/100g adds additional cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit arayes kofta to occasional consumption, request leaner ground meat (chicken or turkey kofta) as a substitute for lamb, avoid added high-sodium condiments, and choose whole wheat pita to improve the dietary fibre profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Ground lamb is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine pathways relevant to RA disease activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute ground lamb with ground chicken or turkey kofta to reduce arachidonic acid load, retain olive oil and parsley for their anti-inflammatory properties, and limit red meat consumption to no more than once per week.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Arayes Kofta (also known as Arayes Kafta or simply Arayes) is a popular Levantine and Middle Eastern dish consisting of spiced ground lamb (kafta or kofta) spread generously inside pita bread, which is then grilled or baked until the bread is crisp and the meat is fully cooked through. The dish originates from the Levant region — particularly Lebanon, Syria, Jordan, and Palestine — and has become widely popular across Turkey, the Gulf states, and among Middle Eastern diaspora communities in Southeast Asia, including Singapore. The name 'arayes' derives from the Arabic word for 'bride,' reflecting the dish's festive and celebratory associations. The kofta filling is typically seasoned with a blend of cumin, paprika, cinnamon, allspice, black pepper, finely chopped onion, and fresh parsley, giving it a warm, aromatic, and mildly spiced flavour profile. The exterior of the pita becomes golden and slightly charred from grilling, while the interior remains moist and richly flavoured from the rendered lamb fat. Nutritionally, arayes kofta provides approximately 220 kcal per 100 g, with 14 g protein, 12 g fat (including 4.8 g saturated fat from lamb), and 14 g carbohydrate from the pita bread. Sodium is 280 mg/100g, making portion control important for hypertensive individuals. The glycaemic index is estimated at 57 (medium) based on white pita bread, though the protein and fat content from the lamb filling attenuate the postprandial glycaemic response. Clinically, arayes kofta is suitable for moderate consumption by most healthy adults but warrants caution for individuals managing hypertension (sodium load), type 2 diabetes (moderate carbohydrate and medium GI), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat and sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (arachidonic acid from red meat). Substituting whole wheat pita and leaner ground meat can significantly improve the nutritional profile.

What is the difference between arayes and shawarma?

confidence_reason: No exact arayes kofta entry exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore, USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values were estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite of cooked ground lamb and white pita bread in an approximate 60:40 ratio. Input values are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on white pita bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite estimate (cooked ground lamb + white pita bread, approximately 60:40 ratio). No exact arayes kofta entry found in HPB Singapore, USDA, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2026-04-16. Input per_100g values retained as they are consistent with the composite estimate. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available from composite estimation): monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, and moisture data for this composite dish preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database as of 2026-04-16. 3. Trans fat 0.2 g/100g is plausible for ground lamb (naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid and vaccenic acid); this is a naturally occurring trans fat from ruminant animals, not industrially produced partially hydrogenated oil. 4. GI estimated at 57 (medium) based on white pita bread analogue from University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Protein and fat from lamb kofta are expected to moderate actual postprandial glycaemic response. 5. The dish is described in the input as 'Turkish' but arayes kofta is more accurately classified as Levantine (Lebanese/Syrian/Palestinian/Jordanian) in culinary origin; it is popular across the broader Middle East including Turkey. Cuisine field updated to 'Middle Eastern / Levantine' accordingly. 6. Multilingual local names (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable for this Middle Eastern dish served in Singapore; the dish does not have established local-language names in these languages. Arabic aliases have been added as the dish's native-language context. 7. No core macro fields are zero; zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
